Expecting unchanging happiness in a changing world will end in disappointment. Accepting our circumstances in life is our duty and wisdom in this world. God’s entire plan for governing the world will be found altogether wise, just, and good. Then let us seize every favorable opportunity for every good purpose and work. The time to die approaches quickly. Thus labor and sorrow fill the world. This is given to us so that we may always have something to do; no one was sent into the world to be idle.

Everything is as God made it, not as it appears to us. We have the world so much in our hearts, are so preoccupied with thoughts and cares of worldly things, that we have neither time nor spirit to see God's hand in them. The world has not only gained possession of the heart, but has formed thoughts against the beauty of God’s works. We mistake if we think we were born for ourselves; no, it is our business to do good in this life, which is short and uncertain; we have little time to be doing good, therefore we should redeem time. Satisfaction with Divine Providence is having faith that all things work together for good to them that love him. God does all that men should fear before him. The world, as it has been, is, and will be. There has no change befallen us, nor has any temptation by it taken us, but such as is common to men.

Without the fear of the Lord, people are worthless; set that aside, and judges will not use their power well. And there is another Judge who stands before the door. With God there is a time for correcting injustices, though we do not see it yet. Solomon seems to express his wish that people might understand that by choosing this world as their portion, they bring themselves to a level with animals, without being free from present troubles and a future judgment, as they are. Both return to the dust from which they were taken. What little reason have we to be proud of our bodies or bodily accomplishments! But because no one can fully comprehend it, few properly consider the difference between the rational soul of people and the spirit or life of animals. The spirit of people goes upward to be judged, and is then fixed in an unchangeable state of happiness or misery. It is as certain that the spirit of animals goes downward to the earth; it perishes at death. Surely their case is lamentable, the height of whose hopes and wishes is that they may die like animals. Let our inquiry be, how can an eternity of existence be an eternity of enjoyment for us? To answer this is the grand design of revelation. Jesus is revealed as the Son of God and the Hope of sinners.
